front: What is the fundamental purpose of a test of significance in the context of large samples?
back: To determine whether an observed effect or difference between a sample statistic and a claimed population parameter is statistically significant (i.e., unlikely to have occurred by random chance alone), or if it is due to sampling variability.

front: For a large sample test concerning a population proportion, what is the formula for the test statistic (z-score)?
back: z = (p̂ - p₀) / √( (p₀(1 - p₀)) / n ) where p̂ is the sample proportion, p₀ is the hypothesized population proportion under the null hypothesis, and n is the sample size.

front: What are the two main approaches to making a decision in a large sample significance test, and what do they involve?
back:
1. Critical Value Approach: Compare the calculated test statistic (z) to a critical value from the standard normal distribution (z*), based on the chosen significance level (α).
2. P-value Approach: Calculate the probability (P-value) of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as, or more extreme than, the one observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. Reject H₀ if the P-value is less than α.

front: Why is the standard normal distribution (z-distribution) used for large sample tests, even when the original population is not normal?
back: Due to the Central Limit Theorem (CLT). For large samples (typically n > 30), the sampling distribution of the sample mean (or proportion) is approximately normally distributed, regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution.

front: What are the typical null (H₀) and alternative (H₁) hypotheses for a two-tailed test of significance regarding a population mean?
back:
H₀: μ = μ₀ (The population mean is equal to the hypothesized value)
H₁: μ ≠ μ₀ (The population mean is not equal to the hypothesized value)
